title: Why Some Species Adapt and Others Disappear

lesson.learn:
Species survive change when they can adapt faster than their environment changes. That can happen through genetic variation, behavioral flexibility, migration, or a niche that gives them room to adjust. Species go extinct when the pressure is too severe, the population too small, or the change too fast for evolution to keep up.

The difference is not simply strength. It is fit. A species that matches its environment can thrive, but a species that loses access to food, habitat, or reproductive success can disappear even if it was once abundant.
lesson.simpler: Some species change fast enough to survive new conditions, while others cannot adapt quickly enough and die out.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Adaptation and extinction depend on whether a species can keep pace with environmental change.
lesson.deeperDive:
Natural selection works on variation that already exists in a population. If climate shifts, predators arrive, or food sources change, the individuals with helpful traits are more likely to survive and reproduce. Over time, that can lead to adaptation. But if the population is too small or the environmental change is too abrupt, extinction becomes more likely.

Darwin’s finches and peppered moths are famous examples of adaptation. Polar bears show how climate change can push a specialist species toward danger. The broader lesson is that survival is often about flexibility, not perfection.
lesson.funFact: A species can be successful for millions of years and still disappear if conditions change too fast.
references: UC Museum of Paleontology - adaptation and extinction

people: Alfred Russel Wallace - independently developed evolution ideas
Conservation biologists - scientists who track extinction risk
places: The Galapagos Islands - classic adaptation example
Industrial Britain - peppered moth selection pressures
The Arctic - habitat under rapid climate change
Biodiversity hotspots - regions where extinction risk is high
years: 1859 - On the Origin of Species is published
19th century - industrial selection becomes visible in moths
21st century - modern climate change raises adaptation pressure
concepts: Natural selection - differential survival and reproduction
Genetic variation - differences within a species
Extinction - permanent loss of a species
Niche - the role a species plays in its environment
Adaptation - a trait that improves survival
